title: Disagreement in GA: Josh Buice is Right. Life Training Institute Woefully Wrong.
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman defends Pastor Josh Buice's support of the Georgia abolition bill against Nathan Apodaca and Life Training Institute, arguing that pro-life criticisms of equal protection and immediatism rest on misconceptions about what abolition bills actually do and on a refusal to confront the failures of the pro-life establishment.
audiences: Abolitionist seeking sharper rebuttals to incrementalism
Pastor or Christian thinker weighing strategy
Legislator considering an equal protection bill
Activist engaging Life Training Institute-style apologetics

questions.question: Do abolition bills actually impose first-degree murder charges on every woman who has an abortion?
questions.answer: No. Silberman argues Apodaca misunderstands the content of these bills. Abolition bills do not single women out for prosecution; they simply remove the legal exception that treats preborn children as less than persons, leaving prosecutorial discretion and existing homicide law to handle each case on its facts.
questions.quote: An abolition bill says nothing of prosecuting women or anyone else. Quite simply, it makes preborn children from the moment of fertilization equal to born people under law. How homicide laws are presently enforced is how prenatal homicide would be enforced upon the passage of an abolition bill.

questions.question: How does Silberman answer the worry that abolition would require unfair, un-nuanced prosecutions?
questions.answer: He concedes the underlying premise that homicide law is fact-sensitive and notes Buice already affirmed it. The objection that abolitionists ignore nuance collapses once one realizes equal protection simply extends ordinary due process to prenatal victims.
questions.quote: Of course "homicide laws have factors that get taken into account during both the investigation and the subsequent prosecution." Buice explained as much earlier in his article: "Every case is unique and deserving of due process under the law."

questions.question: Why does Silberman say pro-life "abortion bans" without criminalization are not real bans?
questions.audience: Legislator considering an equal protection bill
questions.answer: Because the bans only restrict who may perform an abortion while leaving the mother free to procure one herself. With abortion pills available online, those laws cannot honestly be said to have stopped abortion in the states that passed them.
questions.quote: These pro-life "abortion bans" simply regulate who is allowed to perform the abortion: namely these bans all explicitly prohibit an abortionist from helping the mother murder her child, while permitting the mother to do so herself. With the ease of finding and ordering abortion pills online, and the anti-abolitionist, pro-life laws that approve of self-managed abortion, it's impossible to say with confidence that abortion has gone down at all since Roe was overturned.

questions.question: What does the Louisiana case study reveal about who is actually blocking abolition bills?
questions.audience: Activist engaging Life Training Institute-style apologetics
questions.answer: It exposes the pro-life establishment, not hostile cultural institutions, as the decisive obstacle. After committee approval, an open letter from 76 pro-life groups flipped Republican legislators and killed the bill.
questions.quote: The difference was that 76 pro-life organizations wrote an open letter to every State legislator in the country opposing the abolition bill. The pro-life politicians, in many cases, do what the pro-life groups tell them to do. Hollywood, Facebook, Democrats, CNN, and Louisiana State University were all powerless to stop the Abolition of Abortion in Louisiana Act. The bill was sailing through, that is, until the pro-life groups stepped in.

questions.question: Is the claim that women abort out of "well-meaning ignorance" credible?
questions.audience: Pastor or Christian thinker weighing strategy
questions.answer: Silberman calls it indefensible. Anyone who has done sidewalk counseling or campus outreach knows women and pro-aborts are not confused about prenatal humanity; the argument is a poll-tested talking point, not a principled position.
questions.quote: Beckwith and Apodaca know their argument isn't true. It's just a line they repeat to justify a more politically palatable position. It's a poll-tested political talking point masquerading as a principled argument; nonsense masquerading as cunning, nuanced wisdom. America does not have an intellectual problem when it comes to abortion, it has a moral problem.

questions.question: What does Silberman say is wrong with framing abortion in terms of heartbeats or pain capability?
questions.audience: Pastor or Christian thinker weighing strategy
questions.answer: These framings concede the pro-abortion premise that arbitrary biological traits determine human worth, which trains legislators and the public to think humanistically rather than biblically.
questions.quote: Again, heartbeat bills and pain-capable acts are not bringing attention to prenatal humanity. They are training people to continue thinking within the pro-abortion, humanist, functionalist worldview that says arbitrary characteristics are what make us valuable and worthy of protection.

questions.question: How does an incremental bill end up actively harming the cause it claims to advance?
questions.audience: Abolitionist seeking sharper rebuttals to incrementalism
questions.answer: Silberman gives a real-life example: an Ohio state senator's aide told him the senator believed life begins at a heartbeat — a doctrine learned not from Scripture or science but from the heartbeat bill itself.
questions.quote: When I was done, his aide said, "This was very interesting, but it's Senator Brenner's personal religious conviction that life begins at a heartbeat." Where did he learn that ridiculous and evil idea that humans don't have value until their heartbeats can be detected? He learned it from the Heartbeat Bill.

questions.question: What is the actual definition of immediatism Silberman wants critics to understand?
questions.audience: Abolitionist seeking sharper rebuttals to incrementalism
questions.answer: Immediatism is not the expectation that the evil ends overnight; it is a stance about what to demand. Like Garrison, immediatists expect a long fight but refuse to compromise the demand itself.
questions.quote: Immediatism is not overnight-ism. Immediatists are not under the impression that the culture will drastically change overnight or that the object of their protest will be abolished overnight. We understand, as our 19th century abolitionist predecessors did, that the fight to abolish a great societal evil is a struggle that will likely take years.

questions.question: Why does Silberman say incremental demands cannot be grounded in any substantial authority?
questions.audience: Pastor or Christian thinker weighing strategy
questions.answer: Because every line short of conception implies legality on the wrong side of that line, contradicting both Scripture and the Equal Protection Clause; the immediatist demand alone can be defended from God's Word and the Constitution.
questions.quote: An incrementalist's demand for a 20-week abortion ban or a heartbeat bill cannot be defended from God's word or the Constitution, as these laws – by implying the legality of abortion prior to the arbitrary standard they set for who counts as fully human – violate both.

questions.question: What does Silberman argue is the deeper consequence of compromised legislative foundations?
questions.audience: Activist engaging Life Training Institute-style apologetics
questions.answer: Compromise produces no prophetic witness, and without prophetic witness there is no real cultural change. Worse, arbitrary lines actively dehumanize the babies left outside them.
questions.quote: Compromise bills and arguments mean compromised foundations, compromised foundations mean no powerful prophetic witness from our demands, and no powerful prophetic witness from our demands means no positive cultural change from our demands. This is one of the many reasons immediatists demand the immediate, and not the gradual, abolition of the evil.

related.title: Equal Protection
related.reason: Directly addresses the criminalization-of-women objection that Apodaca centers his critique on.

related.title: Immediatism
related.reason: Foundational explanation of the doctrine Silberman defends in the closing section against Apodaca's incrementalism.

related.title: Abolitionist, Not Pro-Life
related.reason: Frames the larger movement-level distinction Silberman is drawing between Buice's abolitionism and LTI's pro-life position.

related.title: Abolition of Abortion in Louisiana Act Passes Committee!
related.reason: Background on the 7-2 committee vote Silberman cites as proof that abolition bills can pass when pro-life groups stand down.

related.title: Rep. Alan Seabaugh and the Louisiana House Ensure Murder by Abortion Continues
related.reason: Documents the floor-vote reversal Silberman uses to indict the pro-life lobbying letter.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Expands the structural case against heartbeat and pain-capable bills that Silberman summarizes.

related.title: Incremental Laws (of All Sorts) Undermine Repentance
related.reason: Develops the argument that compromised legislation produces no prophetic witness or genuine cultural change.
